name: Lisbon: From Commerce Square to the Castle

stops.name: Praça do Comércio
stops.shortDescription: A magnificent, sun-drenched square facing the Tagus River. It was built on the ruins of the old Royal Palace, which was destroyed by the catastrophic 1755 Lisbon earthquake.

stops.name: Rua Augusta Arch
stops.shortDescription: A stone, triumphal arch-like building commemorating the city's reconstruction after the 1755 earthquake. It serves as an ornate gateway into Baixa, the vibrant downtown grid.

stops.name: Santa Justa Lift
stops.shortDescription: A beautifully intricate, 19th-century Neo-Gothic iron elevator created by an apprentice of Gustave Eiffel. It vertically connects the lower streets of the Baixa with the higher Carmo Square.

stops.name: Lisbon Cathedral (Sé)
stops.shortDescription: The oldest church in the city, built in 1147 right after the Christian reconquest of Lisbon. Its fortress-like, Romanesque facade is iconic, complete with rose windows and heavy twin bell towers.

stops.name: Miradouro de Santa Luzia
stops.shortDescription: A romantic observation deck offering sweeping views over the terracotta roofs of Alfama and the Tagus River. The terrace is beautifully decorated with traditional blue and white azulejo tiles.

stops.name: Castelo de São Jorge
stops.shortDescription: A Moorish castle towering majestically over Lisbon's historic center. Walking its ancient, pine-shaded ramparts rewards you with arguably the most breathtaking panoramic views of the entire city.

stops.name: Miradouro das Portas do Sol
stops.shortDescription: A spectacular viewing terrace situated just above Alfama. It provides a postcard-perfect view over the labyrinth of medieval streets tumbling down towards the glittering river.

stops.name: Alfama Labyrinth
stops.shortDescription: The oldest and most traditional neighborhood in Lisbon. Wandering its steep, maze-like alleyways feels like stepping back in time to the days before the great earthquake.

stops.name: Largo do Chafariz de Dentro
stops.shortDescription: A historic, bustling square at the bottom of the Alfama hill. Historically, its large public fountain provided the main source of drinking water for the surrounding neighborhood.

stops.name: Museu do Fado
stops.shortDescription: A beautiful museum dedicated to Fado, the melancholic, deeply emotional musical genre that was born in these very streets. It's essential for understanding Lisbon's poetic soul.

stops.name: Panteão Nacional (National Pantheon)
stops.shortDescription: Housed in the majestic Church of Santa Engrácia, this towering white monument serves as the final resting place for Portugal's most illustrious presidents, writers, and artists.

stops.name: Monastery of São Vicente de Fora
stops.shortDescription: A stunning 17th-century church and monastery known for its magnificent cloisters, which are fully lined with exquisite blue-and-white tiles depicting the fables of La Fontaine.

stops.name: Miradouro da Graça
stops.shortDescription: A popular, lively terrace shaded by pine trees. Locals and tourists alike gather here at an open-air kiosk to enjoy a coffee or beer while admiring the sweeping city views.

stops.name: Miradouro da Senhora do Monte
stops.shortDescription: The highest viewpoint in Lisbon. Tucked away near a small chapel, it offers the most expansive, unobstructed panorama of the castle, downtown Baixa, and the Tagus River.
